stories.headline: Iran strikes Kuwait desalination plant and oil refinery as war expands into Gulf; EU considers fuel rationing
stories.body: Iran's war with the US and Israel spilled further into the Gulf on Friday as a power and desalination plant in Kuwait was hit by an air strike, hours after the Al-Ahmadi oil refinery was targeted by early-morning drone attacks for the third time since the war began. Kuwait blamed Iran, while the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps denied responsibility and accused Israel. In the UAE, at least 12 people were injured by falling debris during missile interceptions in Abu Dhabi. The European Commission is now studying contingency measures including fuel rationing, reduced speed limits, and incentives for public transport and teleworking, with energy commissioner Dan Jorgensen warning that prices will remain elevated for a long time. Separately, more than 100 US-based international law experts signed an open letter condemning the US-Israeli campaign as a violation of the UN Charter and possible war crimes, citing strikes on schools, hospitals and infrastructure, and noting the war costs US taxpayers up to two billion dollars a day.

stories.headline: Burkina Faso's military ruler tells citizens to 'forget about democracy'
stories.body: Captain Ibrahim Traore, who seized power in Burkina Faso in a 2023 coup, declared in a state television interview that democracy kills and the people of the West African country must forget about it. The 38-year-old, who casts himself as a revolutionary leader standing up to Western imperialism, pointed to Libya's post-Gaddafi chaos as an example of what Western-imposed democracy produces. Traore initially pledged to restore democratic rule by July 2024 but extended his junta's mandate by five years, and in January banned all political parties as part of a plan to rebuild the state. He offered no alternative system of governance but said Burkina Faso would build something new rooted in sovereignty and patriotism, with traditional leaders and grassroots structures playing central roles. His government has been accused of punishing critics by sending them to the front lines of the war against Islamist militants.

stories.headline: Myanmar's coup leader Min Aung Hlaing elected president by loyalist parliament
stories.body: Myanmar's military chief Min Aung Hlaing has been elected president by a parliament filled with his loyalists, five years after promising to hold elections and return to civilian rule within a year of his 2021 coup. With the armed forces guaranteed a quarter of parliamentary seats and the military's own party winning nearly 80 percent of the rest in a heavily tilted election, the outcome was preordained. Min Aung Hlaing stepped down as armed forces commander, as required by the constitution, but created a new consultative council to exercise paramount authority over both civilian and military affairs, ensuring he retains full control. He installed hardliner General Ye Win Oo as his replacement at the head of the military. The five years since the coup have been catastrophic for Myanmar, with his decision to use lethal force against mass protests igniting a civil war that has killed thousands, displaced millions and devastated the economy.

stories.headline: Cuba announces pardon of more than 2,000 prisoners as US eases oil blockade
stories.body: Cuba has announced plans to pardon 2,010 prisoners as what it called a humanitarian gesture during Holy Week, amid heightened US pressure on the island. The announcement came days after the Trump administration allowed a Russian tanker to deliver crude oil to the fuel-starved nation, and Russia said it would send a second tanker. Analysts say the releases likely signal that quiet negotiations between Havana and Washington are advancing, though the outcome remains unclear. Cuba did not identify who would be pardoned but said the decision was based on the nature of their crimes, good behaviour, health reasons and time served. The releases will include young people, women, and prisoners over 60 scheduled for early release within the next year. Those convicted of murder, sexual assault, drug crimes and theft are excluded. Releasing political prisoners has long been a core US demand, and Cuba had already freed 51 prisoners in March as a goodwill gesture toward the Vatican.

stories.headline: Storm Erminio lashes Greece and blankets Crete in Saharan dust as global food prices spike
stories.body: Heavy rain, thunderstorms and gale-force winds from Storm Erminio battered much of Greece this week, flooding streets and stranding vehicles across the mainland and several Aegean islands. One person died near Athens after being swept away while escaping a flooding basement. Winds carrying Saharan dust from North Africa turned the skies over Crete a vivid orange-red. Southern Italy also saw significant rainfall with rivers bursting their banks, and up to 1.5 metres of unseasonable snowfall in the southern Apennines. Meanwhile, a UN report found global food commodity prices rose 2.4 percent in March, with vegetable oil and sugar prices jumping 5 and 7 percent respectively, as the Middle East war drives up energy and freight costs. The UN warned that global food prices could average 15 to 20 percent higher in the first half of 2026 if the crisis persists, with about a third of global fertiliser production normally transiting the now-closed Strait of Hormuz.

stories.headline: Trump fires Attorney General Pam Bondi, replaced by former personal lawyer Todd Blanche
stories.body: President Trump abruptly removed Attorney General Pam Bondi from her post on Thursday, making her the second female cabinet member to be fired in recent weeks after Kristi Noem was ousted as homeland security secretary in March. Trump had reportedly grown increasingly frustrated with Bondi's handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files, which she had promised to release upon taking office but which were ultimately published only after Congress passed a law requiring it. As recently as Thursday morning Trump was publicly defending Bondi, but hours later confirmed her departure. She will be replaced by Todd Blanche, her former deputy and Trump's ex-personal lawyer, who denied the Epstein files played any role. Democrats accused the administration of misogyny, noting that only women have lost their jobs while male officials like Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth and FBI director Kash Patel, both mired in controversy, remain in their posts. Both fired women have been replaced by men in what was already the least diverse US cabinet this century.

stories.headline: Hegseth fires Army's top general and two other officers in the middle of the Iran war
stories.body: Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has ousted the Army's top uniformed officer, General Randy George, along with two other generals, without giving any reason for the departures while the United States wages war against Iran. George, who had held the post of Army Chief of Staff since August 2023 under the Biden administration, was told to retire effective immediately. The Pentagon also dismissed Army General David Hodne and Major General William Green. General Christopher LaNeve, who was serving as Hegseth's top military aide, will step in as acting Army Chief of Staff in a meteoric rise from two-star general two years ago. The firings are the latest in a string of more than a dozen dismissals of top generals and admirals by Hegseth since taking office, and come five weeks into the US-Israeli campaign against Iran with no clear timeline from Trump on when the conflict may end.

stories.headline: Trump signs executive order imposing up to 100 percent tariffs on foreign-made pharmaceuticals
stories.body: President Trump signed an executive order on Thursday that could impose tariffs of up to 100 percent on patented drugs if pharmaceutical companies do not reach pricing deals and commit to manufacturing in the United States within the coming months. Companies that have signed most-favoured-nation pricing agreements and are building US facilities will face zero tariffs, while those that have not struck deals will see rates escalate from 20 percent to 100 percent over four years. The EU, Japan, South Korea and Switzerland will see a 15 percent tariff on patented drugs, while the UK gets 10 percent. A senior administration official said companies still have months to negotiate before the highest tariffs take effect, and noted 17 pricing deals have already been reached with major drugmakers. The pharmaceutical trade group PhRMA warned that taxes on cutting-edge medicines will increase costs and could jeopardise billions in US investments. The order arrived on the first anniversary of Trump's Liberation Day tariffs, which were later overturned by the Supreme Court.

stories.headline: President of Wisconsin's largest mosque detained by ICE; supporters allege targeting of pro-Palestinian speech
stories.body: Salah Sarsour, a Palestinian-born legal permanent resident and president of the Islamic Society of Milwaukee, was detained by nearly a dozen ICE agents who surrounded his car on Monday after he left his home. His attorneys say he was held on the grounds of being a foreign policy threat, a claim they dismiss as baseless, and believe he was targeted for speaking out against Israel and for a conviction as a minor by Israeli military courts for allegedly throwing rocks at officers. Sarsour, 53, has lived in the US for more than 30 years with no criminal record, and his wife and four adult children are US citizens. A diverse group of religious leaders and elected officials called for his immediate release, comparing his case to that of former Columbia University activist Mahmoud Khalil, who also faces deportation over foreign policy threat claims. An Evangelical Lutheran bishop called the arrest the latest example of the administration seeking to silence opposition and intimidate those who speak differently.

stories.headline: Iran war clouds Republican midterm prospects as Amazon adds fuel surcharge for sellers
stories.body: Roughly six months before voters begin casting ballots in the 2026 midterms, Republicans are bracing for political backlash over the Iran war's escalating costs and uncertain trajectory. Veteran Republican pollster Neil Newhouse warned of an ugly November, saying the party's edge is being chipped away at the worst possible time. While Republicans once believed they could preserve their narrow House majority and hold the Senate easily, many now privately concede the House is all but lost and Democrats have a realistic shot at the Senate. The Republican National Committee has largely avoided the war in talking points, and vulnerable candidates are sidestepping the issue, unwilling to defend or challenge Trump publicly. Meanwhile, the economic fallout continues to ripple through the economy: Amazon announced a 3.5 percent fuel and logistics surcharge on third-party sellers starting April 17, joining UPS, FedEx and the US Postal Service in passing on rising energy costs to businesses and ultimately consumers.

stories.headline: Left-wing parties seal last-minute deal to run together in Andalusian elections on May 17
stories.body: Spain's left-of-PSOE parties finalised an agreement on Friday morning to run together under the Por Andalucia banner in the May 17 regional elections, after more than 12 hours of negotiations that stretched through the night and into the deadline for coalition registration. Izquierda Unida leader Antonio Maillo will head the Seville list, Sumar's Esperanza Gomez will lead in Cadiz, and Podemos will head the Jaen candidacy. The deal narrowly avoided a repeat of 2022 when Podemos filed its paperwork late, forcing its candidates to appear as independents. The coalition now includes IU, Sumar, Podemos, Iniciativa del Pueblo Andaluz, Alternativa Republicana and Partido Verde Andaluz, though Adelante Andalucia remains outside. The agreement was paradoxical: Podemos's national leadership initially refused to rejoin the coalition, while its Andalusian wing always favoured unity, creating a tense last-minute scramble to resolve what amounted to a crisis within an already-existing alliance.

stories.headline: Woman murdered in Basauri; partner detained as 15th victim of gender violence in 2026
stories.body: A 44-year-old woman was found dead on Wednesday morning at a home in Basauri, in the Basque province of Bizkaia, after what was initially reported as a sudden medical episode. Emergency services were unable to save her life, and a subsequent forensic examination determined the death was violent. The Ertzaintza detained her 45-year-old partner on Friday morning on suspicion of homicide in the context of domestic violence. The case, if confirmed, would mark the 15th gender violence killing in Spain so far this year.

stories.headline: Spain's digital ID app MiDNI now legally equivalent to the physical document
stories.body: Spain's Interior Ministry has confirmed that the MiDNI mobile application is now fully valid as legal identification, meaning all public and private establishments are obliged to accept the digital format as equivalent to the physical DNI card. The app, which had been downloadable for a year, can be used for any legal purpose requiring identification including hotel check-ins, vehicle rentals, notarial proceedings, university registration, pharmacy purchases and exercising the right to vote. The app does not store personal data on the phone but instead queries the national identity database in real time, generating a time-limited QR code. Users can choose three levels of disclosure: age verification only, basic ID data, or the full document. The Partido Popular had demanded the electoral commission suspend the app's use in elections over concerns about QR verification, and the commission agreed to block it and the digital driving licence from voting identification pending further review.

stories.headline: Sumar prepares street mobilizations to save the rent-freeze decree before May deadline
stories.body: Spain's left-wing coalition partner Sumar is preparing an offensive combining political negotiations and street protests to ensure Congress ratifies the government's housing decree, which freezes rent increases at a maximum of 2 percent for contracts expiring before the end of 2027. The decree must be ratified by May 5 or it lapses. The key obstacle is Junts, whose leader Jordi Turull has already declared it will be voted down, accusing the government of criminalising small landlords. Sumar insists the measure primarily affects large property holders like Caixabank and Blackstone, which together own 40,000 rental properties, and warns that without the freeze hundreds of thousands of tenants face unaffordable rent hikes. Sumar took some encouragement from Junts's recent vote in favour of a separate government decree on Iran-related economic measures, seeing it as a sign the Catalan party is willing to negotiate again.

stories.headline: Government expects to reject over 30 percent of migrant regularization applications
stories.body: Spain's Migration Ministry expects to receive around 750,000 applications for the extraordinary migrant regularization programme due to launch this month, but estimates that only about 500,000 people will ultimately be granted legal status after duplicates and non-qualifying applications are filtered out. Of the projected beneficiaries, some 300,000 would enter through a pathway for those who applied for international protection, while the remaining 200,000 would be foreign nationals who can demonstrate five months of continuous residence in Spain. The ministry's internal planning document, obtained by El Pais, also reveals that the 180,000 pending residency applications sitting in the system from before the decree will be resolved automatically through a fast-track mechanism, provided applicants have no criminal record and do not pose a public safety threat. The figures are based on the experience of last year's post-DANA Valencia regularization, where applications exceeded final beneficiaries by 143 percent.

stories.headline: Groundforce ground staff strike disrupts flights at El Prat airport over Easter weekend
stories.body: An indefinite strike by ground handling staff at Groundforce is causing disruptions at Barcelona's El Prat airport on Friday and is set to continue on Monday and Wednesday. Some 1,100 workers at Barcelona and 6,000 across 12 Spanish airports are participating in the action, called by UGT, CCOO and USO unions over what they say is non-compliance with the collective agreement and loss of purchasing power. The Transport Ministry imposed 100 percent minimum services, a decision that infuriated unions who announced they would challenge it before the Constitutional Court. During earlier strike days this week, unions reported widespread delays averaging one hour per flight, cancelled services, thousands of bags left unloaded or delivered late, and pileups of aircraft on the tarmac. The stoppages affect airlines including Air Europa and Lufthansa across three daily time windows.

stories.headline: Rodalies R4 line to reopen next week after Renfe and train driver unions reach deal
stories.body: The R4 commuter rail line between Martorell and Sant Sadurni d'Anoia will reopen next week after Renfe and the main train driver unions reached an agreement on Thursday, ending a standoff that had kept the line closed to commuter services for over two months since a fatal accident at Gelida in January. The deal resolves a dispute over operating schedules that driver unions had refused to approve, initially over safety concerns and later over demands for more positions. Although the double track at Gelida had been operational since March 14 and regional trains were already running, commuter services remained suspended because the Semaf union had not validated the Rodalies timetables. The agreement, backed by CCOO, UGT, Semaf and SF though opposed by CGT and Alferro, will also allow proper scheduling of trains through the Garraf tunnels currently under construction. The Generalitat's transport department must now confirm the exact reopening day, expected to be Tuesday.

stories.headline: Mossos intensify Collserola patrols over Easter to contain African swine fever outbreak
stories.body: The Mossos d'Esquadra and Agents Rurals are stepping up controls around Collserola natural park during the Easter holiday period to contain the African swine fever outbreak that has been active for four months. A third containment barrier is being deployed between Abrera, the B-40 motorway and Terrassa as a precautionary measure. Authorities have extracted more than 3,000 wild boar from the infected zone, significantly reducing the population, though some animals have learned to avoid detection. Police have issued nine fines for people breaching restrictions, including one criminal case in Sant Cugat where someone repeatedly vandalised containment fences, causing a boar that tested positive for the disease to escape the first containment ring. Officials urged the public to avoid walking, cycling or taking pets into the restricted zone, which extends 20 kilometres from Cerdanyola del Valles.

stories.headline: Hotel occupancy nears 90 percent across Catalunya for Easter amid good weather and proximity tourism
stories.body: Hotels and campsites across Catalunya are reporting occupancy rates approaching 90 percent for the Easter holiday, buoyed by the arrival of good weather after months of rain and a boost in proximity tourism partly attributed to the international context of the Iran war. The Costa Daurada and Terres de l'Ebre expect up to 90 percent occupancy with a notable increase in British visitors, while the Costa Brava expects 85 percent thanks to last-minute bookings that have become the norm. The Pyrenees are enjoying an exceptional end to the ski season with fresh snowfall, and adventure sports operators are optimistic about rafting conditions thanks to abundant rainfall. El Prat airport has 11,339 flights scheduled between March 27 and April 6, slightly above last year's Easter figures. Campsites expect to sustain over 80 percent occupancy for the next six weeks thanks to European visitors with longer Easter holidays.

stories.headline: Teaching unions plan week-long strike in May after talks with Catalan government collapse
stories.body: Catalan teaching unions are planning a week of territorialised strikes in May after negotiations with the Generalitat's education department broke down. The USTEC majority union, CGT and Intersindical will consult their members after Easter with several options, the minimum being a full week of action. The unions rejected an agreement signed between the department and the minority unions UGT and CCOO, which represent less than 25 percent of union delegates, calling it a deal of minimums that does not reverse the loss of purchasing power teachers have suffered since the austerity-era cuts. USTEC spokesperson Iolanda Segura said the consultation would be joint and whatever the membership decides would be binding, while Intersindical's Marc Martorell confirmed that a week of strikes is the minimum option on the table. Education minister Esther Niubo, who has been on medical leave for two months, is expected to return on Tuesday.
